content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Don't worry — I ___ (help) you carry those heavy bags to your room.
distractor_rationale: going to describes a pre-planned intention; this is a spontaneous offer made at the moment of speaking
present simple cannot express a spontaneous offer about an immediate future action
past simple refers to a completed past action, not a present offer

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I promise I ___ (call) you as soon as I arrive at the hotel tomorrow.
distractor_rationale: going to signals a pre-formed plan, but 'I promise' marks this as a promise made at the moment of speaking, which requires will
present simple cannot express a promise about a future action
past simple refers to a completed past event, not a future promise

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds — it ___ (rain) very soon.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a prediction about an upcoming event
past simple refers to a completed past event, not a future prediction
present continuous describes an action happening right now, but the rain has not started yet

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The phone is ringing — I ___ (answer) it for you.
distractor_rationale: going to expresses a pre-planned intention; answering the phone right now is a spontaneous decision, which requires will
present simple cannot express a spontaneous decision to act immediately
present continuous describes an action already in progress, but the decision to answer is being made at this moment

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you study hard every day, you ___ (pass) your exam next month.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot be used in the result clause of a first conditional; will is required
going to is not used in the result clause of a first conditional; will is the correct form
past simple refers to a completed past event and cannot express a conditional future result

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I think the new café on the main street ___ (be) very popular with students.
distractor_rationale: present simple states a current fact, not a prediction about the future introduced by 'I think'
past simple refers to a completed past state, not a future prediction
going to requires evidence of a current situation pointing to the future; 'I think' signals a personal opinion-based prediction, which takes will

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: He ___ (not/forget) to bring his passport to the airport tomorrow — he checked his bag twice.
distractor_rationale: 'he'll not' is a non-standard contraction; the correct negative contraction of will is won't
present simple cannot express a future prediction about tomorrow's event
past simple refers to a completed past action, not a future prediction

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: The weather forecast says it ___ (be) sunny all weekend, so let's have a picnic on Saturday.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a forecast about a future period; will is needed
past simple refers to a completed past state and cannot describe next weekend's weather
present perfect describes a past action with a present result, not a future forecast

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: One day, people ___ (travel) to other planets the same way we travel to other cities today.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a prediction about a distant future event signalled by 'one day'
past simple refers to a completed past action and cannot describe a future prediction
going to requires present evidence of an imminent event; 'one day' signals a distant, opinion-based prediction, which takes will

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If the last bus has already gone, we ___ (take) a taxi home together.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot serve as the result clause in a first conditional; will is required
past simple refers to a completed past action and cannot express a conditional future result
going to is not used in the result clause of a first conditional; will is the correct form here

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: Don't worry — I ___ (help) you carry those heavy bags to the car.
distractor_rationale: going to describes a pre-planned intention, but this is a spontaneous offer made at the moment of speaking
present simple cannot express a spontaneous future offer; it describes habits or routines
past simple describes a completed past action, not a present offer about the future

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: I promise I ___ (not / forget) to call you tomorrow morning before I leave.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a future promise; it describes current habits or facts
going to describes a pre-planned intention, not a spontaneous promise made at the moment of speaking
past simple describes a completed past action and cannot be used for a future promise

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: If you leave your lunch outside in this heat, the food ___ (go) bad very quickly.
distractor_rationale: present simple in the result clause omits the required will, making the conditional grammatically incomplete
going to is used for plans or intentions, not for the inevitable result stated in a first conditional clause
past simple describes a completed past event and cannot express the future result of a conditional situation

content.instruction: Complete the sentence with the correct form.
content.prompt: My sister thinks she ___ (not / enjoy) the new job because the hours are very long and the office is far from home.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a future prediction about how someone will feel; it describes current or habitual states
he'll not is an incorrect contraction; the standard negative short form is won't, and the subject pronoun is wrong here
going to requires evidence of a prior plan or visible sign; this is a personal prediction based on opinion, which calls for will

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Don't worry — I ___ help you with those heavy bags.
distractor_rationale: going to is used for planned intentions, not spontaneous offers made in the moment
present simple cannot express a spontaneous offer about the future
past simple refers to a completed past action, not a future offer

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds. I think it ___ rain this afternoon.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a future prediction in this context
is going to is a verb phrase, not a bare auxiliary — the sentence already has 'rain' after the gap, so only a bare modal fits
past simple refers to a past event, not a future prediction

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: I promise I ___ call you as soon as I arrive at the hotel.
distractor_rationale: present continuous describes an action happening now or a fixed arrangement, not a promise about the future
past simple refers to a completed past action, not a future promise
present simple cannot express a future promise in this context

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The phone is ringing. I ___ get it — don't move!
distractor_rationale: going to signals a pre-planned intention, but answering the phone is a spontaneous decision made at the moment of speaking
present simple cannot express a spontaneous decision made at the moment of speaking
past simple refers to a completed past action, not an immediate future decision

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: If the weather is nice on Saturday, we ___ have a picnic in the park.
distractor_rationale: present simple in the result clause omits the required modal will, which is a common learner error in conditional sentences
present continuous cannot express a conditional future result in the main clause of a first conditional
past simple refers to a past event and cannot be the result clause of a first conditional

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Maria is very talented, so she ___ do very well in tomorrow's exam.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot express a future prediction based on current evidence about someone's talent
going to is used when there is visible, immediate evidence or a prior plan; a general opinion about talent calls for will
past simple refers to a completed past action, not a future prediction

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: He ___ be late for the meeting — he never arrives on time.
distractor_rationale: present simple with 'is probably' describes a current state, not a future prediction about the meeting
past simple with 'was probably' refers to a past situation, not a future event
'probably going to' is grammatically incomplete without the auxiliary 'is' before it, making it incorrect

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: Tom said he ___ not come to the party, but I hope he changes his mind.
distractor_rationale: 'he'll' repeats the subject 'he' already in the sentence, making it grammatically redundant and incorrect
'won't' already includes the negative meaning, but the sentence separately states 'not', making 'won't not' a double negative error
'would' shifts the sentence into a conditional or reported-speech context that contradicts the direct future meaning needed here

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: ___ you please open the window? It is very warm in here.
distractor_rationale: 'Do' forms a present simple question and cannot make a polite future request in this context
'Are' begins a present continuous question and does not fit the request structure with a bare infinitive 'open'
'Did' forms a past simple question and cannot refer to a present or future request

content.instruction: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence.
content.prompt: The doctor says the patient ___ need to rest for at least two weeks after the operation.
distractor_rationale: 'is going to' is a two-word phrase that cannot precede the bare infinitive 'need' in this slot without creating a double verb error
'needs' uses present simple and cannot express a future medical prediction about the recovery period
'needed' uses past simple and refers to a past necessity, not a future requirement after the operation

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Don't worry — I help you carry those heavy bags to the car.
content.error_sentence: Don't worry — I help you carry those heavy bags to the car.
content.corrected_sentence: Don't worry — I will help you carry those heavy bags to the car.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds. It rains this afternoon.
content.error_sentence: Look at those dark clouds. It rains this afternoon.
content.corrected_sentence: Look at those dark clouds. It will rain this afternoon.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The phone is ringing — I'm going to answer it.
content.error_sentence: The phone is ringing — I'm going to answer it.
content.corrected_sentence: The phone is ringing — I will answer it.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: I promise I am not going to tell anyone your secret, so you can trust me.
content.error_sentence: I promise I am not going to tell anyone your secret, so you can trust me.
content.corrected_sentence: I promise I will not tell anyone your secret, so you can trust me.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: He'll not come to the party if he has to work late on Friday.
content.error_sentence: He'll not come to the party if he has to work late on Friday.
content.corrected_sentence: He won't come to the party if he has to work late on Friday.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: If the weather is nice tomorrow, we go to the beach after lunch.
content.error_sentence: If the weather is nice tomorrow, we go to the beach after lunch.
content.corrected_sentence: If the weather is nice tomorrow, we will go to the beach after lunch.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: The café is full, so we probably find a table at the restaurant next door instead.
content.error_sentence: The café is full, so we probably find a table at the restaurant next door instead.
content.corrected_sentence: The café is full, so we will probably find a table at the restaurant next door instead.

content.instruction: Find and correct the grammar mistake in the sentence.
content.prompt: You look tired — sit down and I am going to make you a cup of tea.
content.error_sentence: You look tired — sit down and I am going to make you a cup of tea.
content.corrected_sentence: You look tired — sit down and I will make you a cup of tea.

content.prompt: Look at those dark clouds — it ___ (rain) very soon.
distractor_rationale: present simple cannot make a prediction about an imminent future event; it describes regular facts or habits
past simple describes a completed past event, not a future prediction
